request.question: John Delzoppo was the Speaker of the Parliament of Victoria under the governance of which political party?

title: Liberal Party of Australia
Unity Party (South Ossetia)
John Delzoppo
38th Canadian House of Commons seating plan
Scottish National Party
Civic Passion
Unparty: The Consensus-Building Party
Boerestaat Party
Speaker of the National Assembly of South Africa
Karu Jayasuriya
sentences: The Liberal Party of Australia is a major political party in Australia.

Founded in 1945 to replace the United Australia Party (UAP), the broadly centre-right Liberal Party is one of the two major parties in Australian politics, along with the Australian Labor Party (ALP).

The Unity Party (Ossetian: Иудзинад , Georgian: ერთიანობის , Russian: Единство ; officially, the South Ossetian Republican Political Party "Unity") is a major political party with a socially conservative ideology in South Ossetia, a partially recognized Caucasian republic, considered by most countries to be a part of Georgia.
The Unity Party, founded in 2003, supported former President Eduard Kokoity, and was for a decade the largest political party in South Ossetia.
After the 2009 elections, the party held 17 out of 34 seats in South Ossetia's parliament.
It is modeled after and is closely linked to the United Russia party, with which it has signed an inter-party cooperation agreement.
The party is a winner of the 2004 and 2009 parliamentary elections.
John Delzoppo (born 3 December 1931) is an Australian retired politician who was Speaker of the Parliament of Victoria from 1992–1996 as part of the Liberal Kennett Government.
In the Canadian House of Commons seating plan, Members of Parliament are generally seated as a group with members of the caucus of their political party.
The members of the governing party or parties are seated to the right of the Speaker in the House of Commons, while members of opposition parties are generally seated to the left of the Speaker.

Where the governing party or parties have more than half the members of the House, members of the government caucus are seated on the "opposition side", and where the government has less than half of the seats, opposition members are seated on the "government side", as is the case with the current Parliament.
The Scottish National Party (SNP; Scottish Gaelic: "Pàrtaidh Nàiseanta na h-Alba" , Scots: "Scots Naitional Pairtie" ) is a Scottish nationalist and social-democratic political party in Scotland.
The SNP supports and campaigns for Scottish independence.

It is the third-largest political party by membership in the United Kingdom, as well as by overall representation in the House of Commons, behind the Labour Party and the Conservative Party, and is the largest political party in Scotland, where it has the most seats in the Scottish Parliament and most of the Scottish seats in the Parliament of the United Kingdom.
The current party leader, Nicola Sturgeon, has served as First Minister of Scotland since 20 November 2014.
Civic Passion () is a radical, populist, and nativist political party in Hong Kong.

Founded by Wong Yeung-tat as an activist group in 2012, it holds strong "localist" views and "militant" approach of protest, opposes the involvement of the Chinese central government in the governance of Hong Kong and has called for the downfall of the Communist Party of China.
In the 2016 Legislative Council election, the Civic Passion formed an electoral alliance with Wong Yuk-man's Proletariat Political Institute and Chin Wan's Hong Kong Resurgence Order.
Cheng Chung-tai became the only candidate of the alliance elected to the legislature and subsequently took over as the leader of the Civic Passion.
After the election, Cheng reorganised the group into a political party and pulled out from the social activism.
Unparty: The Consensus-Building Party (formerly the People's Senate Party) is a political party in British Columbia.
It was registered on September 20, 2011, and changed its name from People's Senate Party to Unparty in August 2012.
The main plank of the party's platform is the promotion of consensus governance over adversarial party politics.

Unparty wants to see meetings held on political issues at the local level that employ consensus decision-making techniques, and for Members of the Legislative Assembly to bring those policy decisions to the Legislative Assembly of British Columbia in Victoria.
The Boerstaat Party (English: "Boer State Party" ) is a right wing South African political party founded on 30 September 1986 by the late Robert van Tonder.
It was never officially registered as a political party because it was unable to rally 500 persons under one roof, a requirement under South African electoral law for official political party status.
It was never represented in the South African Parliament, neither in the apartheid era nor after democratisation.
In 1989, it joined the Afrikaner Weerstandsbeweging (AWB) in declaring support for Jaap Marais, the leader of the Herstigte Nasionale Party and has worked with the HNP on occasion since.
The party was a charter member of the Afrikaner Volksfront coalition group.
It has also operated with the paramilitary group, the "Boere Weerstandsbeweging" (Boer Resistance Movement) led by Andrew Ford.
The Speaker of the National Assembly presides over the National Assembly of South Africa, the lower house of the Parliament of South Africa.
The speaker is chosen from among the Members of the Assembly at its first sitting following a general election and whenever the office is vacant.
The Speaker acts as a "referee", taking charge of debates to make sure that the MPs can participate freely while keeping to the rules.
The Speaker also has managerial duties to ensure that Parliament runs smoothly.
Each political party in the Assembly elects a chief whip to run its affairs.
The presiding officers, the chief whips, and the Leader of Government Business (the person appointed by the Cabinet to liaise with Parliament) together decide on the programme of work.
Deshabandu Karu Jayasuriya (Sinhalese: කරු ජයසුරිය ; born 29 September 1940) is a Sri Lankan politician who has been Speaker of the Parliament of Sri Lanka since 2015.

Previously he was Mayor of Colombo from 1997 to 1999, Minister of Power and Energy from 2001 to 2004, Minister of Public Administration and Home Affairs from 2007 to 2008, and Minister of Buddha Sasana, Public Administration, and Democratic Governance in 2015.
He served as Chairman of the Leadership Council of the United National Party (UNP), as well as Deputy Leader of the UNP.
He is a Member of Parliament representing the Gampaha District.
He received his early education at Ananda College, Colombo.
Under President Ranasinghe Premadasa's government, Karu Jayasuriya was appointed as Sri Lanka's Ambassador to Germany.
As Speaker of Parliament, he also acts as Chairman of the Constitutional Council.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: John Delzoppo was the Speaker of the Parliament of Victoria under the governance of which political party?
hop1_docs: John Delzoppo | John Delzoppo (born 3 December 1931) is an Australian retired politician who was Speaker of the Parliament of Victoria from 1992–1996 as part of the Liberal Kennett Government.

Parliament of Sierra Leone | Parliament of Sierra Leone is the legislative branch of the government of Sierra Leone.  It is principally responsible for making laws.  The Sierra Leone parliament consists of 124 members, of which 112 members are directly elected from across Sierra Leone's 14 Districts.  The Parliament is led by the Speaker.  The current speaker of Parliament is Sheku Badara Bashiru Dumbuya (APC).  The current elected members of parliament are entirely composed of members of the All People's Congress and the Sierra Leone People's Party, the two largest political parties in Sierra Leone.

38th Canadian House of Commons seating plan | In the Canadian House of Commons seating plan, Members of Parliament are generally seated as a group with members of the caucus of their political party.  The members of the governing party or parties are seated to the right of the Speaker in the House of Commons, while members of opposition parties are generally seated to the left of the Speaker.  Where the governing party or parties have more than half the members of the House, members of the government caucus are seated on the "opposition side", and where the government has less than half of the seats, opposition members are seated on the "government side", as is the case with the current Parliament.

Politics of Liechtenstein | Liechtenstein is a principality governed under a constitutional monarchy.  It has a form of mixed constitution in which political power is shared by the monarch and a democratically elected parliament.  There is a two-party system (though there are two minor parties as well) and a form of representative democracy in which the prime minister and head of government is responsible to parliament.  However the Prince of Liechtenstein is head of state and exercises considerable political powers.

Politics of Luxembourg | The politics of Luxembourg takes place in a framework of a parliamentary representative democratic monarchy, whereby the Prime Minister of Luxembourg is the head of government, and of a multi-party system.  Executive power is under the constitution of 1868, as amended, exercised by the government, by the Grand Duke and the Council of Government (cabinet), which consists of a prime minister and several other ministers.  Usually the prime minister is the leader of the political party or coalition of parties having the most seats in parliament.  Legislative power is vested in both the government and parliament.  The judiciary is independent of the executive and the legislature.

Turkish Parliament Speaker election, 2011 | The Turkish Parliament Speaker elections of 2011 were held on 4 July 2011 to elect the 25th Speaker of the Grand National Assembly.  The elected speaker would preside over the 24th Parliament of Turkey, which was elected in the parliamentary election held on 12 June 2011.  With the governing Justice and Development Party (AKP) holding 327 of the 550 seats (a majority of 104) in the Grand National Assembly, the election of the party's candidate Cemil Çiçek was regarded as an effective certainty.

First Cain Ministry | The First Cain Ministry was the 50th ministry of the Government of Victoria (Australia).  It was led by the Premier of Victoria, John Cain of the Labour Party.  The ministry was sworn in on 14 September 1943, but lasted less than four days.  On 15 September, barely 24 hours after Governor of Victoria Sir Winston Dugan had sworn-in the cabinet, the government was defeated in the Legislative Assembly.  Cain's motion to adjourn the parliament for over a week was defeated by the Country Party and the UAP, and Opposition Leader, Albert Dunstan, moved that Parliament resume the next day, giving notice that he would move a motion of no confidence against Cain's government, confident it would be carried by the CP–UAP alliance.  Cain indicated that he would request a dissolution of parliament from the Governor, but if his request was refused, he would resign as Premier.  On 17 September, Cain visited the Governor who refused his request for a dissolution—Cain then resigned and the Governor commissioned Dunstan to form a government, which was sworn in on Saturday 18 September.
hop1_titles: 38th Canadian House of Commons seating plan
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: passage [1] states John Delzoppo served "as part of the Liberal Kennett Government." Bridge entity: John Delzoppo. Missing fact: none — the passages identify the governing party phrase ("Liberal Kennett Government"). Retrieval directions: open John Delzoppo's biography on Wikipedia or the Parliament of Victoria site to confirm the explicit party label (query: "John Delzoppo" site:wikipedia.org OR site:parliament.vic.gov.au). Clarification: none.
response.hop2_query:
John Delzoppo site:wikipedia.org
John Delzoppo site:parliament.vic.gov.au
hop2_docs: John Delzoppo | John Delzoppo (born 3 December 1931) is an Australian retired politician who was Speaker of the Parliament of Victoria from 1992–1996 as part of the Liberal Kennett Government.

Wikidumper.org | WikiDumper.org is a website created by Clifford A. Pickover that promises to permanently record a snapshot of the "best of the Wikipedia rejects", articles that are slated for deletion at the site.  WikiDumper was launched on November 4, 2006, and accepts user submissions.  Although the site doesn't specify its criteria for inclusion, many of its articles don't cite their sources.  The site has been criticized as likely to be less accurate than Wikipedia.

Boldrup Plantation Archeological Site | Boldrup Plantation Archeological Site is a historic archaeological site located at Newport News, Virginia.  It was the location of a 17th-century plantation and now the site of a modern residential development.  During the 17th century, Boldrup Plantation was owned by Gov. John Harvey (d. 1646), Gov. Samuel Stephens (1629-1669), and Gov. William Berkeley (1605-1677); the wife of the latter two men, Frances Culpeper Berkeley, lived there as well.  The site includes the grave slab of Lt. Col. William Cole, colonial secretary of state, the graves of his second and third wives, and a pit house.  The site was excavated in the 1980s.

Gov. John W. Martin House | The Gov. John W. Martin House (also known as Apalachee) is a historic house in Tallahassee, Florida.  The residence of governor John W. Martin, it is located at 1001 Governor's Drive within the borders of DeSoto Site Historic State Park.  On January 6, 1986, it was added to the U.S. National Register of Historic Places.

.mp | . mp is the Internet country code top-level domain (ccTLD) for Northern Mariana Islands.  There are a few sites related to the Northern Mariana Islands in this domain (such as governmental sites under . gov.mp and a few sites in . org.mp and . co.mp).  The get.mp site allows users to register and manage . mp domains.  The . mp name comes from ISO 3166 which specifies MP as the two letter designation for the Northern Mariana Islands.

Marble Springs | Marble Springs, also known as the Gov. John Sevier Home, is a state historic site in south Knox County, Tennessee, in the southeastern United States.  The site was the home of John Sevier (1745–1815)—a Revolutionary War and frontier militia commander and later the first governor of Tennessee—from 1790 until his death in 1815.  A cabin at the site was once believed to have been Sevier's cabin, although recent dendrochronological analyses place the cabin's construction date in the 1830s, well after Sevier had died.

Australia.gov.au | Australia.gov.au is an Australian public sector website, created by the Government of Australia to provide a single entry point to government in Australia.  It is owned and managed by the Department of Finance, and links to information and services from around 900 Australian Government websites, as well as selected State and Territory government resources.  The site indexes over four million pages from Australian Federal, State and Territory governments, using both metadata and full text indexes.
response.summary_2: John Delzoppo served as Speaker of the Parliament of Victoria under the Liberal Party (the Kennett Liberal government). Passage [1] notes he was Speaker from 1992–1996 "as part of the Liberal Kennett Government," confirming the party affiliation.
